name: Historic Route 66
subtitle: Chicago to Santa Monica — The Mother Road
bestMonths: April–October (avoid peak summer heat in desert sections)

budget.label: Budget
budget.includes: budget motels, fast food + 1 sit-down, gas shared between travelers

midrange.label: Mid-range
midrange.includes: mid-range hotels, casual restaurants, fuel, some paid attractions

premium.label: Premium
premium.includes: boutique hotels, nice restaurants, premium experiences

transportNotes: Drive your own car. 300-400 miles/day maximum. Gas ~$3.85/gal (AAA avg).
highlights: Cadillac Ranch
Petrified Forest NP
Santa Monica Pier
Blue Whale of Catoosa

hotels.name: Best Western Route 66 Rail Haven
hotels.notes: Historic 1938 motor court on Route 66; themed rooms

hotels.name: Route 66 Hotel & Conference Center
hotels.notes: Themed Route 66 pool; larger property

hotels.name: President Abraham Lincoln Springfield Hotel
hotels.notes: Downtown Springfield; DoubleTree by Hilton

attractions.name: Historic Route 66 Begin Sign
attractions.location: Adams St & Michigan Ave, Chicago, IL
attractions.description: Official start of Route 66 — bronze marker at the corner of Adams Street and Michigan Avenue, in front of the Art Institute of Chicago. Essential first photo stop.

attractions.name: Gemini Giant (Launching Pad)
attractions.description: One of four surviving 'Muffler Men' fiberglass giants on Route 66 — a 30-foot astronaut holding a rocket, dating to the 1960s. One of the most iconic early Route 66 stops.
attractions.hours: Always visible (outdoor statue); gift shop hours vary

attractions.name: Route 66 Hall of Fame & Museum
attractions.description: Free museum featuring Route 66 memorabilia, vintage photos, and murals painted on downtown buildings. One of the best free stops in Illinois.

attractions.name: Paul Bunyan Muffler Man
attractions.description: 19-foot fiberglass muffler man holding a hot dog, standing since 1966. Classic quirky Route 66 roadside art.

attractions.name: Abraham Lincoln Presidential Library & Museum
attractions.description: World-class museum dedicated to the 16th president with life-size recreations, original documents, and interactive exhibits. Springfield was Lincoln's home for 24 years.

hotels.name: Moonrise Hotel
hotels.notes: Boutique hotel in the Loop neighborhood; rooftop bar with arch views

hotels.name: Marriott St. Louis Grand
hotels.notes: Historic 1917 grand hotel in downtown St. Louis

hotels.name: Holiday Inn St. Louis - Downtown Conv Ctr
hotels.notes: Reliable mid-range; walking distance to Gateway Arch

attractions.name: Cahokia Mounds State Historic Site
attractions.location: Collinsville, IL (8 miles from St. Louis)
attractions.description: UNESCO World Heritage Site — largest pre-Columbian settlement north of Mexico, with 80+ remaining earthen mounds including Monks Mound (100 feet tall). Grounds open daily. Note: Interpretive Center is undergoing renovations in 2025-2026.
attractions.hours: Grounds open daily dawn to dusk; Gift shop Wed-Sun 9am-3pm; Interpretive Center partially open (under renovation)

attractions.name: Gateway Arch National Park
attractions.description: America's tallest monument at 630 feet. Ride a tram to the top for panoramic views of the Mississippi River. The adjacent museum chronicles westward expansion.

attractions.name: Chain of Rocks Bridge
attractions.description: Historic 1929 bridge that carried Route 66 over the Mississippi River with a unique 22-degree bend in the middle. Now a pedestrian and bike path with spectacular river views.

attractions.name: World's Largest Catsup Bottle
attractions.description: A 170-foot-tall water tower shaped like a Brooks ketchup bottle, built in 1949 and listed on the National Register of Historic Places. A beloved roadside landmark.

hotels.name: Boots Court Motel
hotels.notes: Vintage 1939 Route 66 motel; individually shaped cottages; historic gem

hotels.name: Holiday Inn Express Joplin
hotels.notes: Modern amenities near Route 66 corridor

hotels.name: Baymont by Wyndham Joplin
hotels.notes: Budget-friendly; clean and reliable

attractions.name: Meramec Caverns
attractions.description: Historic limestone caves stretching over 4.5 miles, legendary hideout of outlaw Jesse James. One of the most visited caves in the US. A classic Route 66 barn-advertised attraction.
attractions.hours: Daily; Summer (May-Aug): 8:30am-7:30pm; Spring/Fall: 9am-5/6pm; Winter: 9am-4pm. Closed Thanksgiving & Christmas.

attractions.name: Cuba Murals & Fanning Route 66 Outpost
attractions.description: Cuba is known for large-scale murals celebrating Route 66 history covering building facades downtown. Fanning Outpost features the World's Largest Rocking Chair.
attractions.hours: Always visible (outdoor murals); Outpost hours vary

attractions.name: Wagon Wheel Motel (Historic Landmark)
attractions.description: One of the oldest surviving motels on Route 66, built in 1947. Even if not staying, the stone cottages and vintage neon are worth a photo stop.

attractions.name: 66 Drive-In Theatre
attractions.description: Operating since 1949, this authentic drive-in movie theater still screens films on weekends — a true Route 66 experience.
attractions.hours: Fri-Sun evenings (seasonal, April-September); gates open at dusk

hotels.name: The Skirvin Hilton Oklahoma City
hotels.notes: Iconic 1911 grand hotel; a National Historic Landmark and Route 66 institution

hotels.name: Hard Rock Hotel & Casino Tulsa
hotels.notes: Upscale option if stopping overnight in Tulsa instead

hotels.name: The Campbell Hotel
hotels.notes: Boutique 26-room themed hotel; 1927 building lovingly restored

attractions.name: Blue Whale of Catoosa
attractions.location: Catoosa, OK (22 miles east of Tulsa)
attractions.description: Giant blue whale built in 1972 as a private gift, now a beloved Route 66 icon. Originally a swimming hole; now a photo stop and picnic area on a small lake.

attractions.name: Tulsa Art Deco District & Route 66 Districts
attractions.description: Tulsa claims to be the 'Capital of Route 66' with 8 distinct Route 66 districts. The downtown Art Deco architecture (Boston Avenue Methodist Church, Philcade Building) is spectacular.
attractions.hours: Exterior views always accessible; individual museums vary

attractions.name: Oklahoma Route 66 Museum
attractions.description: Widely regarded as the best Route 66 museum in the country. Eight chronological galleries with authentic vehicles, memorabilia, and immersive exhibits tracing the highway's history from 1926 to decommissioning.

attractions.name: National Cowboy & Western Heritage Museum
attractions.description: World-class museum celebrating the American West with historic art, rodeo exhibits, Native American culture, and an authentic Western town replica.

hotels.name: Big Texan Steak Ranch Motel
hotels.notes: Wild West theme; home of the famous 72-oz steak challenge; pools and longhorn sculptures

hotels.name: Best Western Plus Amarillo East
hotels.notes: Modern amenities; good value; near Cadillac Ranch

hotels.name: Courtyard by Marriott Amarillo Downtown
hotels.notes: Downtown location near Route 66 walking district

attractions.name: Cadillac Ranch
attractions.description: Ten vintage Cadillacs (1949-1963) buried nose-first in a field at the same angle as the Great Pyramid of Giza. Created by the Ant Farm collective in 1974. Visitors encouraged to spray-paint the cars — bring a can.
attractions.hours: Always open (sunrise to sunset; free admission; field access)

attractions.name: Midpoint Cafe & Route 66 Midpoint Marker
attractions.description: The exact halfway point between Chicago (1,139 miles) and Santa Monica (1,139 miles). The Midpoint Cafe is a classic diner serving homemade pie — a mandatory stop.
attractions.hours: Daily 8am-4pm (seasonal hours may vary; call ahead)

attractions.name: Palo Duro Canyon State Park
attractions.location: Canyon, TX (25 miles south of Amarillo)
attractions.description: The 'Grand Canyon of Texas' — 800 feet deep, 120 miles long. Hiking, horseback riding, and a spectacular sunset drive through red rock formations.

attractions.name: American Quarter Horse Hall of Fame & Museum
attractions.description: Celebrates the history of the American Quarter Horse breed with art, artifacts, and interactive exhibits. Free admission.

hotels.name: Hotel Parq Central
hotels.notes: Boutique hotel in a restored 1926 railroad hospital; rooftop bar with Sandia Mountain views

hotels.name: Blue Swallow Motel
hotels.notes: Historic 1939 motel; highest-rated Route 66 motel in the US (5-star, 2,000+ reviews); neon paradise

hotels.name: Motel Safari
hotels.notes: Authentic Route 66 motel; vintage neon; great budget option

attractions.name: Tucumcari Murals & Neon Signs
attractions.description: This small town has preserved one of the best collections of vintage neon signs on Route 66. The Blue Swallow Motel's neon is legendary. Over 20 large murals cover downtown buildings depicting Route 66 history.
attractions.hours: Always visible (outdoor); best after dark for neon

attractions.name: Petrified Forest National Park
attractions.description: Note: This is best visited Day 7 — but if routing through NM, the park spans both states. Features 225 million-year-old petrified wood logs, the Painted Desert, ancient petroglyphs, and the only section of original Route 66 inside a national park.
attractions.hours: Daily 8am-5pm (MST year-round); $25 per vehicle; closed Thanksgiving & Christmas

attractions.name: Old Town Albuquerque
attractions.description: Historic adobe plaza dating to 1706 with galleries, restaurants, and the San Felipe de Neri Church. Surrounded by shops selling authentic Native American jewelry and art.

attractions.name: Sandia Peak Tramway
attractions.description: World's longest aerial tramway — 2.7 miles up to 10,378 feet on Sandia Peak for sweeping views of the Rio Grande Valley and Route 66 corridor below.

hotels.name: Hotel Monte Vista
hotels.notes: Historic 1927 hotel; famous neon sign; haunted history; great downtown location

hotels.name: Weatherford Hotel
hotels.notes: Historic 1900 hotel; three bars on premises; charming and central

hotels.name: Little America Hotel Flagstaff
hotels.notes: Large property; forested setting; good value for the area

attractions.name: Wigwam Motel
attractions.description: Iconic 1950 motel where guests sleep inside concrete teepee-shaped rooms arranged in a semicircle around vintage Studebakers and Buicks. One of only three surviving Wigwam Village motels in the US.
attractions.hours: Check-in office typically 3pm; exterior viewable anytime

attractions.name: Petrified Forest National Park & Painted Desert
attractions.description: 228-million-year-old petrified logs scattered across a surreal landscape; the Painted Desert with bands of red, pink, and purple badlands; original Route 66 highway visible within the park. 28-mile scenic drive.

attractions.name: Standin' on the Corner Park
attractions.description: Commemorating the Eagles song 'Take It Easy' ('standin' on a corner in Winslow, Arizona'). Bronze statue, a painted mural, and gift shop in a charming small Route 66 town.

attractions.name: Meteor Crater Natural Landmark
attractions.location: Winslow, AZ (5 miles south of I-40)
attractions.description: Best-preserved meteorite impact crater on Earth — 4,000 feet wide and 550 feet deep, formed 50,000 years ago. Museum with actual meteorite fragments and Apollo astronaut training exhibits.

attractions.name: Historic Downtown Flagstaff & Lowell Observatory
attractions.description: Flagstaff's walkable downtown has excellent restaurants and bars along Route 66 (Santa Fe Ave). Lowell Observatory — where Pluto was discovered in 1930 — offers nightly telescope viewing.
attractions.hours: Downtown always active; Lowell Observatory daily 10am-10pm (summer); $22 adults

hotels.name: Barstow Route 66 Motel
hotels.notes: Authentic Route 66 property right on the Mother Road

hotels.name: Holiday Inn Express Barstow
hotels.notes: Modern amenities; pool; good pit stop option

hotels.name: Ramada by Wyndham Barstow
hotels.notes: Reliable mid-range; convenient freeway access

attractions.name: Kingman Route 66 Museum & Powerhouse Visitor Center
attractions.description: Housed in a restored 1907 electrical powerhouse. Excellent Route 66 museum inside with vintage vehicles and exhibits. The visitor center has a great Route 66 gift shop.

attractions.name: Oatman Ghost Town & Wild Burros
attractions.location: Oatman, AZ (historic Route 66 detour, Sitgreaves Pass)
attractions.description: 1915 gold rush town with wild burros roaming Main Street, daily gunfight reenactments, and the historic Oatman Hotel where Clark Gable and Carole Lombard honeymooned. One of Route 66's best detours.
attractions.hours: Daily 9am-5pm; gunfights at noon and 3pm on weekends

attractions.name: Needles, CA — Colorado River Entry
attractions.description: California entry point on Route 66 and the Colorado River. The beautifully restored El Garces (1908 Spanish Mission railway hotel) is worth a look. Dramatic desert and river scenery.

attractions.name: Peggy Sue's 50's Diner & Dinosaur Park
attractions.description: Classic 1950s diner with enormous film/TV memorabilia collection and an outdoor park filled with life-size dinosaur statues. A delightful end-of-day stop.

hotels.name: Fairmont Miramar Hotel & Bungalows
hotels.notes: Legendary Santa Monica hotel steps from the pier and Route 66 terminus

hotels.name: Bayside Hotel Santa Monica
hotels.notes: Walk to the pier; boutique property with ocean-adjacent location

hotels.name: Hotel Erwin Venice Beach
hotels.notes: Rooftop bar; hip Venice Beach locale; 2 miles from the pier

attractions.name: San Bernardino Wigwam Motel
attractions.description: The third and most westerly surviving Wigwam Village motel — another chance to see (or sleep in) the concrete teepee rooms if you didn't stop in Holbrook, AZ. Located right on Foothill Blvd / Historic Route 66.

attractions.name: Route 66 Museum, Victorville
attractions.description: California-focused Route 66 museum in a former Casa del Desierto (Harvey House) railroad hotel. Covers the California stretch of Route 66 history with exhibits and vintage vehicles.

attractions.name: Santa Monica Pier — End of the Trail
attractions.description: The official end of Route 66. The famous 'End of the Trail' sign hangs near the pier entrance. Walk to the end of the pier, breathe the ocean air, and celebrate completing 2,448 miles of American history.

attractions.name: Venice Beach Boardwalk
attractions.description: The ultimate California welcome — street performers, bodybuilders at Muscle Beach, artists, and the Pacific Ocean. A perfect celebratory end to the road trip.

name: Western Coast of Italy
subtitle: Cinque Terre to the Amalfi Coast

flightEstimate.notes: Summer peak (Jun-Aug) runs $200-400 higher than shoulder season. Book 6-8 weeks out for best fares. Fly into Rome (FCO) or Milan (MXP/LIN). NYC-Rome is typically the cheapest route from the US.

budget.label: Budget
budget.includes: Hostel dorm or budget hotel ($60-100/night), street food + 1 sit-down trattoria, public transport only, 1-2 paid attractions

midrange.label: Mid-range
midrange.includes: 3-star hotel or B&B ($130-200/night), breakfast at café, lunch at trattoria, dinner at restaurant with wine, occasional taxi, most major attractions

premium.label: Premium
premium.includes: 4-5 star hotel or boutique property ($300-600+/night), fine dining, private tours, skip-the-line tickets to all attractions, private transfers

transportNotes: High-speed trains (Trenitalia Frecciarossa / Italo) are the gold standard between major cities — fast, comfortable, and bookable far in advance for low 'Super Economy' fares. Florence↔Rome takes ~1h30m; Rome↔Naples ~1h10m. Book at trenitalia.com or italotreno.com 4-6 weeks ahead for best prices. Do NOT rent a car in Florence, Rome, or Naples — ZTL restricted zones, no parking, and heavy fines. Rent a car only for the Amalfi Coast day if desired, but ferries are strongly preferred: SITA buses run the SS163 Amalfi Drive but are slow and crowded in peak season. Ferry operators NLG and Alilauro run Sorrento↔Positano↔Amalfi routes (seasonal, April-October).
highlights: Cinque Terre Villages
Uffizi Gallery
Colosseum & Vatican
Pompeii
Amalfi Coast

tripDays.from: Milan Malpensa (fly in) → Milan Centrale → La Spezia Centrale by Intercity train → Cinque Terre Express to Vernazza or Monterosso
tripDays.travelTime: ~2.5-3h total from Milan Centrale to first village

hotels.name: Hotel Villa Steno
hotels.description: Clifftop family-run hotel with terraced garden and sea views. One of the top-rated mid-range options in Monterosso, known for excellent breakfast and panoramic terrace.

hotels.name: Hotel La Colonnina
hotels.description: Charming small hotel in the heart of colorful Vernazza village, walking distance to the harbor and main piazza. Simple, comfortable rooms.

hotels.name: Hotel Porto Roca
hotels.description: Clifftop boutique hotel perched above Monterosso's old town with dramatic sea views and a pool. Premium pick for a splurge night on arrival.

attractions.name: Via dell'Amore (Path of Love)
attractions.description: Iconic 900-meter coastal cliffside path connecting Riomaggiore and Manarola, reopened in 2024 after a 12-year closure. One-way route with guided access. Spectacular sea views.
attractions.price: Included in Cinque Terre Card (€22-35/day). Pre-book timed slot mandatory; max 400 visitors/hour.

attractions.name: Sentiero Azzurro (Blue Trail)
attractions.description: The famous 12km coastal hiking trail connecting all five villages from Riomaggiore to Monterosso. Best completed in sections — the Manarola–Corniglia and Corniglia–Vernazza stretches are most rewarding. Passes through vineyards and dramatic coastal scenery.
attractions.hours: Daylight hours; trails open year-round (some sections close after rain)

attractions.name: Vernazza Harbor & Piazza Marconi
attractions.description: The most picturesque of the five villages, with a natural harbor, colorful tower houses, and outdoor cafes surrounding the main square. Swim off the small beach or climb Doria Castle for panoramic views.

attractions.name: Monterosso al Mare Beach
attractions.description: The only real sandy beach among the five villages, divided into a free section (Fegina beach) and paid beach clubs. Ideal for swimming and sunbathing — the most beach-resort feel in Cinque Terre.
attractions.price: Free public section; beach clubs €20-30/day with sunbed

attractions.name: Manarola Village & Sunset Viewpoint
attractions.description: The most photographed village in Cinque Terre, with tightly packed pastel-colored buildings rising from the sea. The viewpoint above the village offers the iconic Cinque Terre postcard shot, especially golden at sunset.

foodHighlights.name: Ristorante Belforte (Vernazza)
foodHighlights.description: Perched dramatically on the medieval watchtower above Vernazza harbor, serving elegantly simple Ligurian seafood for over 50 years. Reserve the outdoor terrace for views. Local specialty: trofie al pesto and fresh seafood.

foodHighlights.name: Il Frantoio Bakery (Vernazza)
foodHighlights.description: A Cinque Terre institution tucked on a side street. Must-try: farinata (Ligurian chickpea pancake) and pan fritto (fried bread stuffed with stracchino cheese), available from 4pm. Budget-friendly street food.

tripDays.tips: Book Via dell'Amore time slots online at least 1-2 weeks in advance in summer — slots sell out. Buy the Cinque Terre Card (Train + Trekking) at La Spezia station before boarding. Stay in Vernazza or Monterosso for the best village experience; avoid Riomaggiore (most touristy). Trains between villages run every 30-60 minutes (Cinque Terre Express, €5-10 per hop). Arrive by 2pm to beat day-trippers from Genoa and Milan. Summer is brutally crowded July-August; shoulder season (May-June, Sept-Oct) is ideal.

hotels.name: Hotel Villa Steno
hotels.description: Clifftop family-run hotel with terraced garden, sea views, and excellent Ligurian breakfast.

hotels.name: Gianni Franzi Hotel (Vernazza)
hotels.description: Classic Vernazza guesthouse above the famous Trattoria Gianni Franzi restaurant on the harbor piazza. Basic rooms with village atmosphere; book early.

hotels.name: Hotel Porto Roca
hotels.description: Clifftop boutique hotel with panoramic sea views, pool, and award-winning restaurant.

attractions.name: Corniglia Village
attractions.description: The only Cinque Terre village not directly on the water, perched 100m above the sea on a rocky promontory. Reached by climbing 382 steps (Lardarina staircase) or a shuttle bus from the station. Far quieter than other villages with a true local feel.

attractions.name: Manarola to Corniglia Coastal Trail (Sentiero #6)
attractions.description: One of the most scenic segments of the Blue Trail, passing terraced vineyards and sea views. Moderate difficulty. Part of the Sentiero Azzurro network.
attractions.price: Included in Cinque Terre Trekking Card (€10-15)

attractions.name: Vernazza to Monterosso Trail
attractions.description: The most challenging but rewarding trail section, with the highest elevation gains and most dramatic coastal panoramas. Passes ancient terraced vineyards and lookout points.
attractions.hours: Daylight hours (check trail status — may close after heavy rain)
attractions.price: Included in Cinque Terre Trekking Card

attractions.name: Boat Tour of the Five Villages
attractions.description: Take a ferry boat around all five villages for a completely different perspective of the cliffside architecture. Seasonal ferry service connects villages; guided boat tours available from Monterosso.

foodHighlights.name: Trattoria Gianni Franzi (Vernazza)
foodHighlights.description: A beloved Vernazza institution on the main piazza cooking honest Ligurian cuisine since the 1960s. Try the pansoti (stuffed pasta with walnut sauce), trofie al pesto, and catch of the day. Outdoor harbor seating.

foodHighlights.name: Ristorante Miky (Monterosso)
foodHighlights.description: One of Cinque Terre's finest restaurants, near Fegina beach. Known for creative seafood and extensive local wine list featuring Cinque Terre DOC whites. Reserve ahead for outdoor terrace tables.

tripDays.tips: Day 2 is best used for deeper hiking — buy the Trekking Card and tackle Vernazza–Monterosso (hardest, most scenic) in the morning before heat. Afternoon: swim at Monterosso beach. The Cinque Terre Card covers unlimited train hops, all hiking trails, and Via dell'Amore. Local specialties to try: focaccia di Recco, farinata, trofie al pesto, acciughe (anchovies), and the local sciacchetrà dessert wine. Luggage storage available at La Spezia station if arriving with large bags.

tripDays.from: Cinque Terre (La Spezia Centrale) → Florence Santa Maria Novella
tripDays.travelTime: ~2h (regional Trenitalia; change possible at Pisa or direct)

hotels.name: Hotel Davanzati
hotels.description: Highly rated family-run boutique hotel in a 14th-century palazzo. Walking distance to Uffizi, Duomo, and Ponte Vecchio. Outstanding reviews for service. Often sells out — book early.

hotels.name: Hotel Spadai
hotels.description: Modern boutique hotel steps from the Cathedral. Stylish, comfortable rooms and excellent central location for exploring on foot.

hotels.name: Soprarno Suites
hotels.description: Beautifully designed boutique property in the artisan Oltrarno district, across the Arno from the main sights. Ideal for travelers who want a more local feel away from tourist crowds.

attractions.name: Uffizi Gallery
attractions.description: The world's greatest collection of Renaissance art. Home to Botticelli's Birth of Venus, Michelangelo's Tondo Doni, Leonardo da Vinci, Raphael, and Caravaggio. The Medici family's former administrative offices, now housing masterworks spanning 700 years. Allow 3-5 hours minimum.
attractions.hours: Tue-Sun 8:15-18:50 (last entry 17:30); closed Mondays, Jan 1, May 1, Dec 25
attractions.price: €20 advance online (+ €4 booking fee); first Sunday of month free

attractions.name: Cathedral of Santa Maria del Fiore (Duomo) & Brunelleschi's Dome
attractions.description: Florence's defining skyline icon. The cathedral itself is free; climb Brunelleschi's extraordinary 15th-century dome for sweeping 360° city views. Also includes the Campanile (bell tower) and Baptistery of San Giovanni with its famous bronze 'Gates of Paradise'.
attractions.price: Cathedral free. Combined ticket (Dome + Campanile + Baptistery + Museum): €30, valid 72h. Advance booking required for dome.

attractions.name: Accademia Gallery — Michelangelo's David
attractions.description: Home to Michelangelo's original 17-foot marble David (1504), one of the most iconic artworks in human history. Also houses his unfinished 'Prisoners' series. Compact museum — perfect for a focused 2-hour visit.
attractions.price: €12 advance (+ €4 booking fee); first Sunday of month free

attractions.name: Ponte Vecchio
attractions.description: Florence's famous medieval bridge spanning the Arno, lined with jewelry shops. The only bridge spared by German forces in WWII. Stunning at golden hour and sunset. The Vasari Corridor (private elevated walkway) passes overhead.

attractions.name: Palazzo Pitti & Boboli Gardens
attractions.description: The Medici family's Renaissance palace now houses six museums. The Boboli Gardens (one of Italy's finest formal gardens) offers a peaceful escape with sculptural grottos and city views from the hilltop. Combined ticket best value.
attractions.price: Combined ticket with Uffizi €40 (3-day validity); Boboli Gardens only €10

foodHighlights.name: Trattoria Mario (San Lorenzo)
foodHighlights.description: Florence's most beloved communal-table lunch spot, open since 1953 and still packed with locals daily. Via Rosina 2 (near Mercato Centrale). Cash only, no reservations. Arrive by 11:45 to avoid a long queue. Renowned for hearty Tuscan classics: ribollita, pasta e fagioli, bistecca alla fiorentina.

foodHighlights.name: All'Antico Vinaio
foodHighlights.description: Florence's most famous schiacciata sandwich shop (Via dei Neri 65r), with massive focaccia sandwiches piled with Tuscan cured meats, cheeses, and spreads. Expect a queue — worth it. Perfect for a quick, cheap, incredible lunch between museums.

tripDays.tips: Day 3 is a travel/orientation day. Arrive Florence mid-afternoon, check in, then do Ponte Vecchio and Oltrarno neighborhood walk at sunset. Reserve Uffizi and Accademia online NOW — both sell out weeks in advance in summer. The Duomo dome also requires advance timed booking. The historic center is extremely walkable; major sights cluster within a 15-minute walk. Tuesday is best for Accademia (David) as it's slightly quieter.

hotels.name: Hotel Davanzati
hotels.description: Family-run boutique hotel in a 14th-century palazzo, 5 min walk to Uffizi.

hotels.name: Hotel Laurus al Duomo
hotels.description: 4-star hotel with panoramic rooftop terrace overlooking the Duomo. Spacious rooms, excellent value for the location.

hotels.name: Portrait Firenze
hotels.description: Salvatore Ferragamo's luxury boutique hotel in a 15th-century palazzo overlooking the Arno. Florence's best hotel for pure design and location.

attractions.name: Uffizi Gallery (book morning slot)
attractions.description: World-class Renaissance art collection. Botticelli, Raphael, Michelangelo, Caravaggio, Leonardo. Enter at 8:15 opening for the quietest experience.

attractions.name: Accademia Gallery — Michelangelo's David
attractions.description: Michelangelo's masterpiece and the greatest Renaissance sculpture. Also see his unfinished 'Prisoners' emerging from marble blocks.

attractions.name: Piazzale Michelangelo Sunset Viewpoint
attractions.description: The famous hilltop terrace south of the Arno offering the most iconic panoramic view of Florence — the Duomo, Campanile, and red-roofed cityscape stretching to Fiesole hills. Arrive 45 minutes before sunset for the best light. Free, accessible by foot (uphill 20-min walk from center) or bus #13.

attractions.name: Mercato Centrale & San Lorenzo Market
attractions.description: Florence's magnificent iron-and-glass covered market (1874). Ground floor: fresh produce, meat, cheese — a feast for the eyes and nose. Upper floor: excellent food hall with sit-down Florentine specialties (tripe, lampredotto, wine). Best lunch option in the San Lorenzo neighborhood.
attractions.hours: Ground floor: Mon-Sat 7:00-14:00. Upper food hall: daily 10:00-24:00

attractions.name: Church of San Miniato al Monte
attractions.description: One of the finest Romanesque churches in Tuscany, perched above Piazzale Michelangelo on the same hilltop. Green-and-white marble façade, beautiful mosaic, and Gregorian chant sung by monks at Vespers (17:30 daily). Peaceful alternative to crowded city churches.

foodHighlights.name: Trattoria Sostanza (near Santa Maria Novella)
foodHighlights.description: Via del Porcellana 25. One of Florence's oldest restaurants (est. 1869), famous for its butter chicken (pollo al burro) and the definitive bistecca alla fiorentina. Wood-paneled walls and communal marble tables. Reservations strongly recommended.
foodHighlights.hours: Lunch & dinner Mon-Fri, dinner only Sat; closed Sun

foodHighlights.name: Il Santo Bevitore (Oltrarno)
foodHighlights.description: Via Santo Spirito 64r. One of Florence's best wine bars and dinner spots across the Arno. Creative Tuscan dishes with a stellar natural wine list. Great for a relaxed evening meal in the local Oltrarno neighborhood.

tripDays.tips: The Uffizi and Accademia are your priority for the morning (book 8:15 entry for both). The Duomo dome climb is best on Day 3 or 4 as the queue opens before other museums. For the bistecca alla fiorentina (Florence's signature T-bone, minimum 2 portions), restaurants typically serve it rare (al sangue) — specify if you want it more cooked. Gelato tip: Vivoli near the Uffizi is a century-old institution. Avoid touristy restaurants around Piazza della Signoria for dinner — walk 2-3 blocks away for better value.

hotels.name: Hotel Davanzati
hotels.description: Family-run boutique near all major sights.

hotels.name: Continentale Hotel
hotels.description: Stylish design hotel literally steps from Ponte Vecchio, part of the Lungarno Collection. Rooftop bar with Arno views.

attractions.name: Bargello National Museum
attractions.description: Florence's underrated sculpture museum in a 13th-century palazzo (the city's first public building). Houses Donatello's bronze David (the first freestanding nude since antiquity), Michelangelo's Bacchus, and Cellini's famous salt cellar model. Far fewer crowds than the Uffizi.

attractions.name: Oltrarno Artisan Quarter Walk
attractions.description: Cross the Ponte Vecchio into the Oltrarno — Florence's 'Left Bank' neighborhood of artisan workshops, family-run trattorias, and the less-touristed local side of the city. Walk Via Maggio for antique shops, peek into leather ateliers, and explore Piazza Santo Spirito (best aperitivo spot in Florence).

attractions.name: Boboli Gardens & Palazzo Pitti
attractions.description: The Medici palace complex across the Arno. The 16th-century formal gardens are among Italy's finest, with grottos, fountains, and sweeping views back toward the city. Inside the palace: Silver Museum, Porcelain Museum, and Palatine Gallery with Raphael masterpieces.
attractions.hours: Tue-Sun 8:15-18:30; closed Tuesdays in winter and first/last Mondays monthly
attractions.price: Boboli only €10; combined Pitti + Uffizi + Boboli €40 (3-day ticket)

attractions.name: Basilica di Santa Croce
attractions.description: Florence's 'Pantheon' — the Franciscan basilica where Michelangelo, Galileo, Machiavelli, and Dante are buried or memorialized. Beautiful Gothic interior with Giotto frescoes. Adjacent Pazzi Chapel is an architectural jewel.

foodHighlights.name: Buca dell'Orafo (near Uffizi)
foodHighlights.description: Via dei Girolami 28r. Classic Florentine trattoria in a rustic cellar near the Uffizi, serving no-frills Tuscan classics since generations. Try crostini fiorentini, ribollita, bistecca, and the house wine. A reliable, authentic pick after a morning at the museums.

foodHighlights.name: Enoteca Buca Mario (Piazza Ottaviani)
foodHighlights.description: One of Florence's oldest restaurants (est. 1886) in a Renaissance-era cellar. Classic Florentine cooking — trippa, lampredotto (tripe sandwich), pappa al pomodoro. Excellent for a proper sit-down dinner.

tripDays.tips: Day 5 can be used for any missed sights or a half-day Tuscan day trip: Siena (~1.5h by bus), Pisa (~1h by train, see the Leaning Tower), or the Chianti wine country by rental car. If staying in Florence: early dinner, then evening stroll along Lungarno Torrigiani for the prettiest sunset view of the Ponte Vecchio. The Oltrarno neighborhood is far more relaxed than the centro storico — dinner here is 20-30% cheaper for the same quality.

tripDays.from: Florence Santa Maria Novella → Roma Termini by high-speed train

hotels.name: Hotel Artemide
hotels.description: Elegant 4-star hotel on Via Nazionale with stylish rooms, excellent breakfast, and rooftop. Walking distance to Colosseum, Trevi Fountain, and Spanish Steps. Excellent value for central Rome.

hotels.name: Capo d'Africa Hotel
hotels.description: Intimate boutique hotel just steps from the Colosseum with a stunning rooftop terrace and pool. Ideal location for exploring the ancient sites on foot.

hotels.name: Hotel Campo de' Fiori
hotels.description: Charming hotel in a 16th-century building above the famous outdoor market square. Rooftop terrace with 360° Rome views. Excellent location between Campo de' Fiori, Piazza Navona, and the Jewish Quarter.

attractions.name: Pantheon
attractions.description: The world's best-preserved ancient Roman building (125 AD). The coffered concrete dome with its famous oculus remains an engineering marvel after 1,900 years. Now an active Catholic church. Uncrowded in the morning.
attractions.price: €5 (recently started charging; free on religious holidays)

attractions.name: Piazza Navona & Campo de' Fiori
attractions.description: Rome's most beautiful baroque piazza — Piazza Navona hosts Bernini's Fountain of the Four Rivers. A 10-minute walk away, Campo de' Fiori holds a lively morning produce market (Mon-Sat). Both are perfect for evening aperitivo.

attractions.name: Trastevere Neighborhood Walk
attractions.description: Rome's most atmospheric medieval neighborhood across the Tiber — cobblestone alleys, ivy-covered buildings, and local trattorias. Perfect for a late afternoon or evening wander. Visit the Basilica di Santa Maria in Trastevere (Rome's oldest church).
attractions.hours: Always open; Santa Maria in Trastevere: 7:30-21:00

foodHighlights.name: Da Armando al Pantheon
foodHighlights.description: Salita de' Crescenzi 31, near the Pantheon. Family-owned since 1961, serving top-notch Roman classics at moderate prices. Essential for cacio e pepe, carbonara, amatriciana. Book in advance — tiny, always busy with knowledgeable locals.

foodHighlights.name: Tonnarello (Trastevere)
foodHighlights.description: Three locations in Trastevere. Extensive indoor and heated outdoor seating. Classic Roman cucina: supplì (fried rice balls), pasta carbonara, coda alla vaccinara. Lively, casual atmosphere good for groups. No reservations — arrive early.

tripDays.tips: Day 6 is arrival/orientation day in Rome. Arrive Rome Termini by noon; check in and do an afternoon walk through the historic center (Pantheon → Piazza Navona → Campo de' Fiori → Trastevere for dinner). Book Colosseum tickets NOW online at ticketing.colosseo.it — they sell out weeks in advance in summer. Also book Vatican Museums in advance at biglietteriamusei.vatican.va. Consider taking the Metro from Termini to Colosseo station (Line B, €1.50) to orient yourself. The walk from Colosseum to Pantheon to Piazza Navona is about 45 minutes and passes most major ancient sites.

hotels.name: Hotel Artemide
hotels.description: Elegant 4-star with excellent rooftop, walking distance to Colosseum.

hotels.name: The First Roma Arte
hotels.location: Via del Babuino (between Spanish Steps and Piazza del Popolo)
hotels.description: Stylish design hotel with art gallery vibe, in Rome's luxury shopping district. Perfect for accessing Borghese Gallery and Vatican.

attractions.name: Colosseum (Flavian Amphitheater)
attractions.description: Rome's most iconic monument — the largest amphitheater ever built (72 AD), once hosting gladiatorial combat before 50,000 spectators. Pre-book online; combined ticket includes Roman Forum and Palatine Hill (valid 24h). Allow at least 2 hours inside.
attractions.hours: Daily 8:30-19:15 (summer); last entry 18:15. Closed Dec 25 and Jan 1.
attractions.price: €18 (combined with Forum/Palatine, online). Book at ticketing.colosseo.it

attractions.name: Roman Forum & Palatine Hill
attractions.description: The civic heart of ancient Rome — senate house, triumphal arches, sacred temples, and processional roads. Palatine Hill rises above with the ruins of imperial palaces and sweeping views over the Forum and Circus Maximus. Included in Colosseum ticket.

attractions.name: Trevi Fountain (Fontana di Trevi)
attractions.description: Rome's most famous baroque fountain (1762), designed by Nicola Salvi. Legend: throw a coin over your left shoulder with your right hand to ensure a return to Rome. Beautifully illuminated at night. Go very early morning (7am) or late evening to avoid the worst crowds.

attractions.name: Borghese Gallery
attractions.description: Rome's most intimate great museum, housed in the Cardinal Borghese's 17th-century villa in Villa Borghese park. Holds Bernini's extraordinary marble sculptures (Apollo and Daphne, The Rape of Proserpina) and early Caravaggio paintings. Strictly timed 2-hour entries with limited visitors.
attractions.price: €15 (+ €2 reservation fee). MUST book online at tosc.it weeks in advance — sells out

foodHighlights.name: Checchino dal 1887 (Testaccio)
foodHighlights.description: Via di Monte Testaccio 30. Roman institution for traditional cucina povera — carbonara, coda alla vaccinara (oxtail stew), and offal dishes in a historic cellar. Beloved by serious food lovers. Book ahead.

foodHighlights.name: Bonci Pizzarium (near Vatican)
foodHighlights.description: Via della Meloria 43. Gabriele Bonci's legendary pizza al taglio (by the slice) — widely considered the best pizza in Rome, possibly in Italy. Order by weight; expect inventive seasonal toppings. Standing room only, takeaway. Essential.

tripDays.tips: Morning at Colosseum + Forum (arrive at 8:30 opening to beat heat and crowds). Afternoon: Trevi Fountain, Spanish Steps, and the Villa Borghese park walk. If you booked Borghese Gallery, do it as a timed afternoon visit. For the best Colosseum experience, add the Arena Floor access (extra €5) to see where gladiators stood. Never buy tickets from touts at the gate — always pre-book online. The tourist day pass for buses/metro is €7 but most central sights are walkable. Stay hydrated — summer Rome is brutally hot; free water at nasoni drinking fountains throughout the city.

hotels.name: Hotel Artemide
hotels.description: Excellent 4-star centrally located between Termini and the major sights.

hotels.name: Residenza Paolo VI
hotels.description: Former convent turned boutique hotel with a rooftop terrace directly overlooking St. Peter's Basilica — the most dramatic view in Rome. Ideal base for Vatican day.

attractions.name: Vatican Museums & Sistine Chapel
attractions.description: One of the world's greatest museum complexes: 54 galleries, the Gallery of Maps, Raphael Rooms, and culminating in Michelangelo's Sistine Chapel ceiling (1508-1512). Allow 3-4 hours minimum. Book timed entry weeks in advance to avoid 3-hour queues.
attractions.hours: Mon-Sat 9:00-18:00 (last entry 16:00); closed Sundays except last Sunday of month (free, 9:00-14:00)
attractions.price: €20 (+ €4 booking fee online). Book at biglietteriamusei.vatican.va

attractions.name: St. Peter's Basilica & Dome
attractions.description: The world's largest Christian church and the spiritual heart of Catholicism. Michelangelo's dome, Bernini's bronze baldachin, and his St. Peter's Square colonnade. The basilica itself is free; climb the dome for panoramic Rome views (elevator partway + 320 steps to top).
attractions.price: Basilica free. Dome: €6 (elevator + stairs) or €4 (stairs only)

attractions.name: Castel Sant'Angelo
attractions.description: Former mausoleum of Emperor Hadrian (139 AD), converted to a papal fortress and connected to the Vatican by the covered Passetto di Borgo corridor. Now a museum with papal apartments, armories, and the best panoramic terrace in central Rome with sunset views over the Tiber.

attractions.name: Jewish Quarter & Largo di Torre Argentina
attractions.description: Rome's oldest and most atmospheric neighborhood, home to a 2,000-year Jewish community. Visit the stunning Synagogue museum, wander Via del Portico d'Ottavia, and stop at Largo Argentina — the ancient Republican temples where Julius Caesar was assassinated in 44 BC (now a cat sanctuary).
attractions.hours: Always open. Synagogue/Museum: Mon-Thu 10:00-17:00, Fri 10:00-14:00, Sun 10:00-16:00

foodHighlights.name: Flavio al Velavevodetto (Testaccio)
foodHighlights.description: Via di Monte Testaccio 97/99. Beautiful, hidden-gem trattoria in the Testaccio neighborhood, built into a cave of ancient pottery shards. Superb Roman classics — cacio e pepe, rigatoni alla pajata, excellent house wine. Reserve ahead.

foodHighlights.name: Supplì Roma (various locations)
foodHighlights.description: Rome's famous fried rice balls stuffed with ragù and mozzarella. The ultimate Roman street food. Supplì al Telefono (with stretchy mozzarella) is the classic version. Try at the original Supplì Roma on Via di San Francesco a Ripa in Trastevere.

tripDays.tips: Vatican day: book the earliest morning slot (9am) to arrive at the Sistine Chapel before tour groups. After the Vatican, walk south to Castel Sant'Angelo for the terrace view, then across Piazza Navona. Evening in Trastevere for dinner. The free first Sunday at Vatican Museums draws enormous crowds — avoid if possible or arrive 2h early. Dress modestly for St. Peter's Basilica (covered shoulders and knees required — checked at entrance). Rome's Metro is limited (only 3 lines) so use buses or walk between central sights.

tripDays.from: Roma Termini → Napoli Centrale by high-speed train, then Circumvesuviana to Pompeii
tripDays.travelTime: ~1h10m Rome to Naples by Frecciarossa/Italo; then ~40 min Circumvesuviana to Pompei Scavi

hotels.name: Hotel Romeo
hotels.description: Sleek design hotel by architect Kenzo Tange steps from the port and Castel Nuovo. Rooftop infinity pool with Vesuvius views, Michelin-starred restaurant. Naples' most stylish mid-range/premium option.

hotels.name: Hotel Piazza Bellini
hotels.description: Boutique hotel in a 17th-century palazzo in Naples' vibrant historic center, surrounded by archaeological remains and the city's best street food. Rooftop terrace. Excellent value.

hotels.name: Decumani Hotel de Charme
hotels.description: Former Cardinal's residence on Naples' famous Spaccanapoli street. Beautiful courtyard, classic Neapolitan interiors. Perfect location for pizza, street food, and the archaeological museum.

attractions.name: Pompeii Archaeological Park
attractions.description: The ancient Roman city (established ~6th century BC) buried and preserved under 4-6 meters of volcanic ash by Mount Vesuvius's eruption in 79 AD. Covers 66 hectares with 45+ accessible. Highlights: the Forum, House of the Faun (with Alexander Mosaic replica), Villa of the Mysteries (extraordinary frescoes), Stabian Baths, Lupanar (brothel with erotic frescoes), and the haunting plaster casts of victims in the Garden of the Fugitives. Allow 4-6 hours minimum.
attractions.hours: Daily 9:00-19:00 (Apr-Oct, last entry 17:30); 9:00-17:00 (Nov-Mar, last entry 15:30). Closed Jan 1, May 1, Dec 25
attractions.price: €18 adults (online advance purchase strongly recommended in summer). Free first Sunday of month. Combined ticket with Herculaneum available.

attractions.name: National Archaeological Museum of Naples (MANN)
attractions.description: One of the world's most important archaeology museums, housing virtually all artifacts excavated from Pompeii and Herculaneum — including the Secret Cabinet (erotic art), the original Alexander Mosaic, extraordinary frescoes, and 300,000+ items from ancient Greece, Rome, and Egypt. A must before or after Pompeii.
attractions.hours: Wed-Mon 9:00-19:30 (last entry 18:30); closed Tuesdays and Dec 25
attractions.price: €20 (children under 18 free; free first Sunday of month)

attractions.name: Spaccanapoli Street & Historic Center
attractions.description: The arrow-straight ancient Roman road that literally 'splits Naples' (decumano) — lined with baroque churches, palaces, pizza shops, street altars, and an unforgettable sensory overload. UNESCO World Heritage Site. Walk from Piazza del Gesù to Via San Biagio dei Librai for the quintessential Naples experience.

attractions.name: Piazza del Plebiscito & Royal Palace
attractions.description: Naples' grand central square dominated by the neoclassical Basilica of San Francesco di Paola (modeled on Rome's Pantheon) and the Royal Palace of Naples. The Royal Palace houses 30 decorated rooms of Bourbon-era splendor. The piazza is free; best viewed in the evening.
attractions.hours: Piazza: always open. Royal Palace: Wed-Mon 9:30-18:30; closed Tuesdays

foodHighlights.name: Pizzeria Gino Sorbillo (Spaccanapoli)
foodHighlights.description: Via dei Tribunali 32. The most famous pizza address in the city that invented pizza. 21 generations of the Sorbillo family. The Margherita DOP (San Marzano tomatoes, buffalo mozzarella) is the benchmark. Expect a queue — no reservations. Go at opening (12:30) or after 14:00.

foodHighlights.name: Nennella (Quartieri Spagnoli)
foodHighlights.description: Vico Lungo Teatro Nuovo 103-104. A Naples institution in the Spanish Quarter — noisy, theatrical, and serving massive portions of traditional cucina napoletana (ragù, pasta e fagioli, fritto misto) at remarkably cheap prices. A show and a meal in one. Cash only.

tripDays.tips: Day 9 is ambitious: Naples arrival + Pompeii. Take the Circumvesuviana from Napoli Centrale to 'Pompei Scavi–Villa dei Misteri' station (40 min, €3.20). Arrive Pompeii by 9:00 opening to beat both crowds and heat — afternoon in July-August at Pompeii is brutal (no shade, 35°C+). Return to Naples by 16:00 for the city. If staying in Sorrento instead (better Amalfi Coast base for Day 10): from Naples, take Circumvesuviana to Sorrento (~1h, €4.50). Naples itself is chaotic, loud, and wonderful — don't judge it too quickly; the food alone justifies the visit. Watch for pickpockets in the historic center and near the train station.

tripDays.from: Naples or Sorrento → Amalfi Coast by ferry or car/bus
tripDays.travelTime: Sorrento to Positano by ferry: ~40 min. Positano to Amalfi: ~20 min. Amalfi to Ravello: ~20 min by taxi/car
tripDays.overnight: Positano or Sorrento (end point for homeward journey)

hotels.name: Hotel Poseidon
hotels.description: Family-run hotel perched on the cliffs above Positano with a panoramic pool and sea-view terraces. Mid-range gem with the most sought-after Positano view. Book 3-4 months ahead for summer.

hotels.name: Hotel Buca di Bacco
hotels.description: Historic landmark directly on Positano's main beach, operating since 1916. Restaurant with beach terrace. Iconic Positano experience at a more accessible price than 5-star peers.

hotels.name: Grand Hotel Excelsior Vittoria
hotels.description: Sorrento's legendary 5-star, perched on cliffs above the Bay of Naples since 1834. Wagner and Sophia Loren slept here. Lemon gardens, infinity pool, and direct views of Vesuvius. Splurge finale to the trip.

attractions.name: Positano — Spiaggia Grande & Village Stroll
attractions.description: The Amalfi Coast's most photographed town — cascading pastel buildings from the clifftop down to a pebble beach. Spend the morning at Spiaggia Grande (main beach, with paid sun loungers) or the quieter Fornillo Beach (15 min walk west). The village is best explored by wandering its steep staircases and ceramic-shop-lined streets.

attractions.name: Church of Santa Maria Assunta, Positano
attractions.description: Positano's iconic 13th-century church with its gleaming yellow-green majolica-tiled dome. Houses a Byzantine Black Madonna icon from the 12th century. The crypt contains the Museo Archeologico Romano — remains of a 1st-century BC Roman villa buried by the Vesuvius eruption.

attractions.name: Amalfi Town & Cathedral of Sant'Andrea
attractions.description: The historic maritime republic that gave the coast its name. The Cathedral of Sant'Andrea (12th century, Arab-Norman architecture) dominates the central piazza with its zebra-striped façade and cloister. Nearby: the Museo della Carta (Paper Museum, €3) in a working 13th-century mill — Amalfi invented European paper-making.
attractions.price: Cathedral €3 (cloister); free for church entry. Paper Museum €3

attractions.name: Ravello — Villa Rufolo & Villa Cimbrone
attractions.description: Perched 350m above the sea, the hilltop town of Ravello offers the most dramatic views on the entire Amalfi Coast. Villa Rufolo (11th century) hosts the famous Ravello Festival (July-September). Villa Cimbrone's 'Terrace of Infinity' with its dramatic cliff-edge views and classical busts is one of Italy's most breathtaking viewpoints.
attractions.hours: Villa Rufolo: daily 9:00-sunset. Villa Cimbrone: daily 9:00-sunset

attractions.name: Path of the Gods (Sentiero degli Dei)
attractions.description: The Amalfi Coast's most spectacular hike — a 7.8km trail from Agerola (or Bomerano) to Nocelle/Positano, traversing the ridge 600m above the sea with staggering coastal panoramas. Moderate difficulty (mostly flat ridge trail, steep descent to Positano). The most rewarding hike on the entire Amalfi Coast.
attractions.hours: Daylight hours (no access restrictions; morning start recommended)

foodHighlights.name: Da Adolfo (Laurito Beach, Positano)
foodHighlights.description: A legendary beach restaurant accessible only by free boat from Positano. Serves grilled fish, mozzarella melted on lemon leaves, and fresh pasta on a secluded pebble beach. One of the most magical dining experiences on the coast. Open lunch only, summer only. Reservations essential.

foodHighlights.name: Trattoria da Emilia (Sorrento)
foodHighlights.description: Via Marina Grande 62, Sorrento's fishing harbor. Simple wooden tables directly on the water serving just-caught seafood — grilled fish, pasta alle vongole, linguine allo scoglio. The most authentic and affordable seafood in Sorrento. Arrive early for lunch.

tripDays.tips: The Amalfi Coast drive (SS163) is stunning but genuinely stressful to drive — narrow, sheer drop on one side, frequent tour buses. If self-driving, rent a small car and start extremely early (before 8am) to beat traffic. Ferries are far more relaxing and give the best coastal views: from Sorrento, take NLG ferries to Positano (€15-22), then onward to Amalfi (€10-15). For Ravello: no ferry — take the SITA bus from Amalfi (€1.30, 30 min) or a taxi (~€25). This is Italy's most expensive region: a simple beach lunch in Positano costs €30-50/person. Positano parking is nearly impossible and hugely expensive — always arrive by boat or bus. End the day in Sorrento for easy Circumvesuviana/ferry connections home.

name: Northern Parks Route
subtitle: Chicago to Los Angeles via Badlands, Yellowstone & the National Parks
bestMonths: April–October (avoid peak summer heat in desert sections)

budget.label: Budget
budget.includes: budget motels, fast food + 1 sit-down, gas shared between travelers

midrange.label: Mid-range
midrange.includes: mid-range hotels, casual restaurants, fuel, some paid attractions

premium.label: Premium
premium.includes: boutique hotels, nice restaurants, premium experiences

transportNotes: Drive your own car. 300-400 miles/day maximum. Gas ~$3.85/gal (AAA avg).
highlights: Mount Rushmore
Old Faithful
Zion NP
Bryce Canyon
Badlands

hotels.name: Graduate Madison
hotels.notes: Hip hotel near UW campus and Capitol Square; Michigan Avenue views

hotels.name: Hilton Madison Monona Terrace
hotels.notes: Connected to Frank Lloyd Wright's iconic Monona Terrace convention center; lake views

hotels.name: Hampton Inn & Suites Madison Downtown
hotels.notes: Reliable; central location; free breakfast

attractions.name: Millennium Park & Chicago Lakefront
attractions.description: Start with Chicago's crown jewel — Cloud Gate ('the Bean'), Crown Fountain, and Lurie Garden. Walk the lakefront path along Lake Michigan before heading north.

attractions.name: Wisconsin State Capitol
attractions.description: Stunning Beaux-Arts capitol building with the largest granite dome in the world. Free guided tours available. The surrounding Capitol Square has excellent restaurants and a famous Saturday farmers market.

attractions.name: Frank Lloyd Wright's Monona Terrace
attractions.description: Rooftop community center designed by Frank Lloyd Wright, jutting out over Lake Monona. The rooftop garden has spectacular views. Tours available.
attractions.hours: Daily 8am-9pm; rooftop garden free; tours $6 adults

attractions.name: University of Wisconsin Memorial Union Terrace
attractions.description: Iconic lakeside terrace on Lake Mendota — one of the most beloved gathering spots in the Midwest. Great sunset views, live music, and famous Union butter pecan ice cream.

hotels.name: Hilton Garden Inn Sioux Falls Downtown
hotels.notes: Downtown location near the Falls Park

hotels.name: Holiday Inn Express & Suites Sioux Falls
hotels.notes: Reliable; pool; near I-90 and I-29 interchange

hotels.name: Sheraton Sioux Falls
hotels.notes: Full-service hotel; fitness center; good restaurant

attractions.name: Mall of America (brief stop)
attractions.description: The largest mall in the US — worth a quick stop for the indoor theme park (Nickelodeon Universe with 27 rides), aquarium, and spectacle if you haven't been. Just off I-35W south of Minneapolis.

attractions.name: Falls Park
attractions.description: The Big Sioux River cascades over 25 feet of quartzite rock outcroppings in the heart of the city. The viewing tower offers panoramic views. A beautiful natural setting in an urban park.

attractions.name: Wall Drug Store
attractions.description: Legendary highway landmark that advertised its free ice water for 1,000 miles in each direction. Now a 76,000 sq ft tourist emporium with a Western art museum, backyard (free) with Mount Rushmore replica and dinosaur, and restaurant. Kitschy American road trip perfection.

roadTripDays.hotelPriceNote: Peak summer (July) rates; avoid Sturgis Motorcycle Rally week (early Aug) when rates triple

hotels.name: The Rushmore Hotel & Suites (BW Premier)
hotels.notes: Best Western Premier; central Rapid City location; high-end for the area

hotels.name: Cambria Hotel Rapid City
hotels.notes: Modern boutique property; highly rated; near attractions

hotels.name: Comfort Inn & Suites Rapid City near Mt Rushmore
hotels.notes: Good value; free breakfast; convenient to Black Hills

attractions.name: Badlands National Park — Loop Road
attractions.description: The 39-mile Badlands Loop Road (Hwy 240) winds through otherworldly eroded spires, buttes, and fossil beds. Stop at the Big Badlands Overlook, Door and Window trails (easy), and Fossil Exhibit Trail. Pronghorn and bison are commonly spotted.
attractions.hours: Park open 24/7; Visitor Center daily 8am-5pm; $35 per vehicle (7 days)

attractions.name: Wall Drug Store
attractions.description: Legendary highway landmark advertising free ice water. Now 76,000 sq ft of Western kitsch, art galleries, and restaurants. The backyard (free) has a dinosaur and Mount Rushmore replica.

attractions.name: Crazy Horse Memorial
attractions.description: The world's largest mountain carving in progress — Crazy Horse on horseback, begun in 1948. Already taller than all four heads at Mount Rushmore. The Indian Museum of North America is outstanding.

attractions.name: Custer State Park Wildlife Loop Road
attractions.description: 18-mile loop through open grasslands where free-roaming bison herds of 1,500+ animals are nearly guaranteed to be seen. One of the best wildlife viewing experiences in the lower 48 states.

hotels.name: The Cody Hotel
hotels.notes: Modern upscale hotel; stunning mountain views; pool

hotels.name: Chamberlin Inn
hotels.notes: Historic 1903 inn where Ernest Hemingway stayed; beautiful courtyard

hotels.name: AmericInn by Wyndham Cody
hotels.notes: Clean and comfortable; reliable mid-range option

attractions.name: Mount Rushmore National Memorial
attractions.description: The 60-foot granite faces of Washington, Jefferson, Roosevelt, and Lincoln — one of America's most iconic monuments. The Avenue of Flags, sculptor's studio, and Presidential Trail (0.6 miles) provide context. Evening lighting ceremony in summer.
attractions.hours: Daily 5am-11pm; Sculptor's Studio 9am-6pm; free parking $10; no entrance fee

attractions.name: Devils Tower National Monument
attractions.description: America's first national monument — a 1,267-foot igneous rock tower that juts dramatically from the Wyoming plains. The 1.3-mile Tower Trail circumnavigates the base. Featured in Close Encounters of the Third Kind.
attractions.hours: Daily 24 hours; Visitor Center 8am-7pm (summer); $25 per vehicle

attractions.name: Buffalo Bill Center of the West
attractions.description: World-class complex of five museums: Buffalo Bill Museum, Whitney Western Art Museum, Plains Indian Museum, Draper Natural History Museum, and Firearms Museum. Could spend a full day here.
attractions.hours: Daily 8am-6pm (May-Sept); reduced hours other months; $25 adults

roadTripDays.driveHoursNote: Short drive day — spend most of the day in Yellowstone
roadTripDays.hotelPriceNote: Yellowstone in-park lodges are the most desirable but book 12+ months ahead. West Yellowstone, MT has more options.

hotels.name: Old Faithful Inn (in-park)
hotels.notes: Historic 1904 log structure; largest log building in the world; must book 12+ months ahead

hotels.name: Canyon Lodge & Cabins (in-park)
hotels.notes: Central park location; near Grand Canyon of Yellowstone; modern renovated cabins

hotels.name: Yellowstone Park Hotel (West Yellowstone, MT)
hotels.notes: Just outside west entrance; good value and availability

attractions.name: Old Faithful Geyser
attractions.description: Erupts every 44-125 minutes (average 90), shooting 3,700-8,400 gallons of boiling water 100-180 feet high. Viewing benches fill 30 minutes before predicted eruption times (posted at visitor center).
attractions.hours: Always visible; Old Faithful Visitor Education Center daily 8am-8pm (summer)

attractions.name: Grand Prismatic Spring
attractions.description: The largest hot spring in the US and third-largest in the world — 370 feet wide, with rainbow-colored rings of orange, yellow, and green thermophile bacteria. Walk the 0.9-mile boardwalk loop and take the Grand Prismatic Spring Overlook Trail for aerial views.
attractions.hours: Open daily 24 hours (boardwalk); best in morning light

attractions.name: Grand Canyon of the Yellowstone & Lower Falls
attractions.description: The Yellowstone River plunges 308 feet over Lower Falls into a 20-mile canyon of yellow and orange rhyolite. Artist Point is the iconic overlook — one of the most photographed spots in the national park system.

attractions.name: Mammoth Hot Springs Terraces
attractions.description: Otherworldly cascading travertine terraces formed by hot springs — constantly changing architecture of white and orange calcium carbonate formations. The upper and lower boardwalk loops cover 2 miles.

attractions.name: Lamar Valley Wildlife Viewing
attractions.description: Called the 'American Serengeti' — the best place in North America to see wolves, bison, grizzly bears, elk, and pronghorn. Dawn and dusk are prime viewing times. Bring binoculars.

hotels.name: Grand America Hotel
hotels.notes: The finest hotel in Salt Lake City; full service; excellent location

hotels.name: Marriott City Center Salt Lake City
hotels.notes: Downtown location; walking to Temple Square; well-rated

hotels.name: Hyatt Place Salt Lake City Downtown
hotels.notes: Good value downtown; free breakfast; modern rooms

attractions.name: Grand Teton National Park (en route)
attractions.description: A brief south-through route passes through Grand Teton — the jagged 13,770-foot Teton peaks rising dramatically from the flat Jackson Hole valley. Even a 30-minute stop at Schwabacher Landing or Jenny Lake Overlook is memorable.
attractions.hours: Park open 24 hours; $35 per vehicle (7 days)

attractions.name: Jackson, WY — Town Square
attractions.description: The classic Western mountain town with arched elk-antler gateways at the Town Square, excellent restaurants, galleries, and the iconic Million Dollar Cowboy Bar. Worth a 1-2 hour stop.
attractions.hours: Town square always accessible; shops/restaurants 10am-9pm typical

attractions.name: Great Salt Lake & Bonneville Salt Flats (preview)
attractions.description: The Great Salt Lake is visible from Salt Lake City. The famous Bonneville Salt Flats (where land speed records are set) are 100 miles west on I-80 — best experienced on Day 7 during the drive west.

attractions.name: Temple Square & Utah State Capitol
attractions.description: The 35-acre headquarters of the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints with impressive architecture and grounds. The nearby Utah State Capitol offers panoramic city and mountain views.
attractions.hours: Temple Square exterior daily; Utah Capitol Mon-Fri 8am-8pm, Sat-Sun 8am-6pm

roadTripDays.hotelPriceNote: Springdale hotels fill quickly and command premium summer rates due to proximity to Zion

hotels.name: Cable Mountain Lodge
hotels.notes: Walking distance to Zion shuttle; pool; stunning canyon views from rooms

hotels.name: Desert Pearl Inn
hotels.notes: On the Virgin River; beautiful setting; shuttle stop adjacent

hotels.name: Hampton Inn & Suites Springdale
hotels.notes: Reliable; shuttle stop; good mountain views; free breakfast

attractions.name: Arches National Park
attractions.description: Over 2,000 natural stone arches including the iconic Delicate Arch (1.5 miles round trip) and Landscape Arch (longest in the park). The 18-mile scenic drive passes Windows Section, Balanced Rock, and Fiery Furnace. No timed entry required in 2026.
attractions.hours: Daily 24 hours; park road 7am-8pm typical; $35 per vehicle (7 days)

attractions.name: Bryce Canyon National Park
attractions.description: Thousands of orange-pink hoodoo spires fill 12 natural amphitheaters. Sunrise Point and Sunset Point provide the iconic views. The 5.5-mile Navajo Loop / Queens Garden combination trail descends into the hoodoos.
attractions.hours: Daily 24 hours; Visitor Center 8am-8pm (summer); $35 per vehicle (7 days)

attractions.name: Zion National Park — Angels Landing & The Narrows
attractions.description: Angels Landing: 5.4-mile round trip with 1,488-foot elevation gain to a narrow spine 1,500 feet above the canyon floor. Requires a permit (lottery system). The Narrows: wade up the Virgin River through a 2,000-foot slot canyon — no permit needed for the bottom-up day version.
attractions.hours: Park open 24 hours; Visitor Center 8am-7pm (summer); $35 per vehicle (7 days); Angels Landing permit required ($6 + $6 per person)

attractions.name: Zion Canyon Scenic Drive
attractions.description: Private vehicles are prohibited in the main canyon beyond the Zion Lodge in summer — take the free shuttle through towering Navajo Sandstone walls to stops including Court of the Patriarchs, Zion Lodge, and Temple of Sinawava.
attractions.hours: Shuttle daily 6am-11pm (summer); free with park admission

roadTripDays.hotelPriceNote: Vegas hotel prices vary enormously by day of week and events — weekdays much cheaper than weekends

hotels.name: Aria Resort & Casino
hotels.notes: Modern luxury; central Strip location; world-class amenities

hotels.name: The Venetian Resort Las Vegas
hotels.notes: All-suite hotel; canal recreations; excellent value for the opulence

hotels.name: Park MGM Las Vegas
hotels.notes: Non-smoking casino; modern design; great mid-range Strip option

attractions.name: Valley of Fire State Park (en route)
attractions.description: Nevada's oldest state park — brilliant red Aztec sandstone formations, ancient petroglyphs, and the famous Wave formation. A 90-minute detour from Zion to Las Vegas that's absolutely worth it.
attractions.hours: Daily 6am-7pm (visitor center); park open 24 hours; $15 per vehicle

attractions.name: The Las Vegas Strip at Night
attractions.description: Walk the 4.2-mile Strip to experience the Bellagio fountains (8pm-midnight every 15-30 min), the High Roller observation wheel (550 feet), and the neon spectacle of America's entertainment capital. The Fremont Street Experience in downtown is also spectacular.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours; Bellagio fountains every 15-30 min from 3pm/8pm

roadTripDays.driveHoursNote: Add 1-2 hours for I-10/I-15 LA traffic

hotels.name: Hotel Figueroa Los Angeles
hotels.notes: Historic 1926 hotel near Staples Center; rooftop pool with downtown views

hotels.name: JW Marriott Los Angeles L.A. LIVE
hotels.notes: Upscale; adjacent to entertainment district; excellent amenities

hotels.name: The Line Hotel Los Angeles (Koreatown)
hotels.notes: Hip boutique; great food program; central location

attractions.name: Mojave National Preserve (en route)
attractions.description: Drive through the otherworldly Kelso Dunes (650-foot sand dunes you can climb), lava tube caves, and Joshua tree forests. The Kelso Depot is a beautifully restored 1924 Spanish Colonial railroad depot with a free exhibit.
attractions.hours: Open 24 hours; Kelso Visitor Center 10am-4pm (Thu-Mon); free admission

attractions.name: Getty Center
attractions.description: Richard Meier's travertine hilltop campus houses one of the world's great art collections — Impressionists, European masters, and photography — with spectacular LA skyline and Pacific Ocean views. Admission is free.
attractions.hours: Tue-Fri & Sun 10am-5:30pm, Sat 10am-9pm; Closed Mon; parking $20

attractions.name: Griffith Observatory
attractions.description: Iconic 1935 observatory on the south face of Mount Hollywood with free public telescope viewing and a planetarium. The terrace offers the most famous views of the Hollywood Sign and the LA basin.
attractions.hours: Tue-Fri 12pm-10pm, Sat-Sun 10am-10pm; free admission; shows extra

name: Pacific Coast Highway
subtitle: Los Angeles to Seattle — The Ultimate Coastal Drive

budget.label: Budget
budget.includes: Motels, packed lunches, free beaches & parks

premium.label: Premium
premium.includes: Boutique hotels, fine dining, Hearst Castle, whale watching

transportNotes: ~1355 miles total. Gas prices are notably higher in California — fill up before entering Big Sur (no gas for 60+ miles).
highlights: Drive the Big Sur coastline on Highway 1, considered one of the world's most scenic roads
Tour Hearst Castle, a 165-room hilltop estate overlooking the Pacific
Visit the Monterey Bay Aquarium, one of the finest marine science institutions in the world
Walk among ancient coastal redwoods, some over 2,000 years old and 300 feet tall
Explore San Francisco: Golden Gate Bridge, Alcatraz, Fisherman's Wharf, and world-class dining
See wild elephant seals hauled out on beaches at Piedras Blancas
End in Seattle with Pike Place Market, the Space Needle, and Pacific Northwest seafood

hotels.name: Hotel Californian
hotels.notes: Spanish Colonial design, steps from Stearns Wharf

hotels.name: Courtyard Downtown
hotels.notes: Walk to State Street

hotels.name: Motel 6 Beach
hotels.notes: Budget option near beach

attractions.name: Santa Monica Pier
attractions.description: Iconic pier at the terminus of Route 66 with Pacific Park amusement rides, aquarium, and sunset views over the Pacific. Walk the promenade before leaving LA.
attractions.entranceFee: Free to enter pier; rides require tickets

attractions.name: Rincon Beach
attractions.description: World-class surf break just south of Santa Barbara, popular with surfers and spectators. A great first taste of California coastal scenery.

attractions.name: Santa Barbara State Street and Waterfront
attractions.description: Spanish Colonial Revival downtown with boutiques, restaurants, and the historic mission. Walk the pier at Stearns Wharf and enjoy the red-tile rooftop skyline.
attractions.entranceFee: Free to explore; Santa Barbara Mission $12 adults

roadTripDays.tips: Drive north (LA to Seattle) so the ocean is always on your left, giving you passenger-side views on the most scenic stretches

roadTripDays.to: San Luis Obispo via Pismo Beach
roadTripDays.overnight: San Luis Obispo or Pismo Beach, CA

hotels.name: Mid-range hotel in San Luis Obispo or Pismo Beach, CA
hotels.notes: Search Booking.com for current availability

attractions.name: Solvang
attractions.description: A quirky Danish village in the Santa Ynez Valley, just off Highway 154. Famous for windmills, Danish bakeries, wine tasting, and an ostrich farm. A fun 90-minute detour.

attractions.name: Oceano Dunes State Vehicular Recreation Area
attractions.description: The only place in California where you can legally drive your car on the beach. Vast sand dunes meet the Pacific near Pismo Beach.

attractions.name: Morro Rock
attractions.description: A 576-foot volcanic plug rising dramatically from the bay at Morro Bay. One of the Nine Sisters volcanic peaks. Walk the embarcadero and watch sea otters in the harbor.

roadTripDays.tips: Highway 1 through Big Sur can close without warning due to slides and fires; check Caltrans (dot.ca.gov) before departure and have an alternate route ready via US-101

roadTripDays.to: Big Sur via Hearst Castle and San Simeon

hotels.name: Ripplewood Resort
hotels.notes: Classic cabins along the Big Sur River; rustic

hotels.name: Big Sur Campground Cabins
hotels.notes: Book months ahead — very limited lodging in Big Sur

hotels.name: Ventana Big Sur
hotels.notes: Ultra-luxury resort in the redwoods

attractions.name: Hearst Castle
attractions.description: Media magnate William Randolph Hearst's 165-room hilltop estate set on 127 acres with Roman-style pools, European antiques, and formal gardens. Accessible only by guided tour via shuttle from the visitor center. One of California's most visited landmarks.
attractions.hours: Open daily 9am-4pm; closed Thanksgiving, Christmas, and New Year's Day

attractions.name: Piedras Blancas Elephant Seal Rookery
attractions.description: A free roadside viewpoint 7 miles north of San Simeon where thousands of northern elephant seals haul out on the beach. Year-round viewing; peak season Dec-Feb for births and Feb-March for juveniles.

attractions.name: Bixby Creek Bridge and Point Sur Lighthouse
attractions.description: Bixby Bridge is one of the most photographed bridges in California, a 714-foot single-span arch over a dramatic canyon. Point Sur Lighthouse sits on a 361-foot volcanic rock and offers guided tours on weekends.
attractions.hours: Bridge always visible; Point Sur tours Sat-Sun, typically 10am
attractions.entranceFee: Bridge free; Point Sur tours $15 adults

roadTripDays.tips: Big Sur has extremely limited accommodations and no cell service; book lodging 3-6 months ahead and download offline maps

hotels.name: Portola Hotel & Spa
hotels.notes: Best mid-range, near Fisherman's Wharf

hotels.name: Comfort Inn Monterey
hotels.notes: Budget-friendly, short drive from aquarium

attractions.name: Monterey Bay Aquarium
attractions.description: World-famous aquarium on historic Cannery Row featuring living kelp forests, sea otters, open-sea exhibits with bluefin tuna and sharks, and a popular jellies gallery. One of the top aquariums on earth. Buy tickets well in advance.

attractions.name: 17-Mile Drive
attractions.description: A scenic toll road through the private Pebble Beach community offering views of the Pacific, world-famous golf courses, and the iconic Lone Cypress tree. Best driven in the morning or late afternoon.

attractions.name: Carmel-by-the-Sea
attractions.description: A charming village with no street addresses, fairy-tale cottages, art galleries, and excellent restaurants. Visit Carmel Beach at sunset and walk Ocean Avenue for galleries and wine tasting rooms.

roadTripDays.tips: Muir Woods and Fern Canyon require advance vehicle reservations; book on recreation.gov as soon as your dates are set

hotels.name: Hotel Zephyr Fisherman's Wharf
hotels.notes: Nautical theme, walk to Alcatraz ferry

hotels.name: Proper Hotel Hayes Valley
hotels.notes: Upscale boutique in central SF

hotels.name: HI SF City Center Hostel
hotels.notes: Best budget option; private rooms available

attractions.name: Santa Cruz Beach Boardwalk
attractions.description: California's oldest surviving amusement park, open since 1907. Features classic wooden roller coasters, carnival games, and a boardwalk on the beach. Great stop on the way north.
attractions.hours: Seasonal; summer daily, spring and fall weekends. Typically 11am-10pm summer
attractions.entranceFee: $5 ride-all-day pass or pay-per-ride; free to walk the boardwalk

attractions.name: Golden Gate Bridge and Presidio
attractions.description: Walk or cycle across the 1.7-mile bridge for sweeping views of the bay and city. The Presidio offers trails, a visitor center, and views of the bridge from below at Fort Point.
attractions.hours: Pedestrian path open 5am-9pm daily; cycling 24 hours
attractions.entranceFee: Free to walk; $9.50 toll for vehicles southbound

attractions.name: Fisherman's Wharf and Alcatraz Island
attractions.description: Pier 39 and Fisherman's Wharf offer clam chowder in sourdough bowls, sea lion colonies, and bay views. Book Alcatraz Island day or night tours well in advance through the National Park Service ferry service.
attractions.hours: Ferries from Pier 33 depart multiple times daily; Alcatraz open 9am-4:30pm
attractions.entranceFee: Alcatraz ferry and audio tour: $43 adults, $26 children ages 5-11

roadTripDays.tips: The Bay Area and Portland have major traffic during weekday rush hours (7-9am and 4-7pm); plan drives accordingly

hotels.name: Hotel Zephyr Fisherman's Wharf
hotels.notes: Nautical theme, walk to Alcatraz ferry

hotels.name: Proper Hotel Hayes Valley
hotels.notes: Upscale boutique in central SF

hotels.name: HI SF City Center Hostel
hotels.notes: Best budget option; private rooms available

attractions.name: Muir Woods National Monument
attractions.description: A 554-acre old-growth coastal redwood forest just 12 miles north of the Golden Gate Bridge. Towering trees up to 258 feet high and 1,000 years old. Free shuttle from Sausalito recommended; vehicle reservations required year-round.
attractions.entranceFee: $15 per person adults; under 16 free; vehicle reservation $9

attractions.name: Mission Dolores, Castro District, and Haight-Ashbury
attractions.description: Explore SF's historic neighborhoods. Mission Dolores (1776) is the oldest intact building in SF. Haight-Ashbury preserves the spirit of the 1960s Summer of Love. The Castro is vibrant with rainbow flags, shops, and restaurants.
attractions.entranceFee: Mission Dolores $7 adults; neighborhoods free to explore

attractions.name: Ferry Building Marketplace
attractions.description: A stunning Beaux Arts ferry terminal converted into a gourmet food hall with local artisan vendors, oyster bars, and a weekly farmers market (Tue, Thu, Sat). Perfect for food lovers seeking the best of California produce and restaurants.
attractions.hours: Open daily; market Tuesdays, Thursdays, and Saturdays

roadTripDays.tips: Hearst Castle is only accessible by pre-purchased guided tour and shuttle bus from the visitor center; book tickets at hearstcastle.org

hotels.name: Curly Redwood Lodge
hotels.notes: Historic — built from a single ancient redwood tree

hotels.name: Lighthouse Inn
hotels.notes: Clean mid-range, walk to Battery Point Lighthouse

attractions.name: Humboldt Redwoods State Park and Avenue of the Giants
attractions.description: A spectacular 31-mile alternate route along the original Highway 101 through the world's largest remaining stand of old-growth coast redwoods. Pass through groves of 300-foot giants and stop at the Founders Tree and Big Tree Area.

attractions.name: Fern Canyon, Prairie Creek Redwoods State Park
attractions.description: A magical slot canyon with 50-foot walls draped entirely in five-fingered ferns, with a creek running through the bottom. Featured in Jurassic Park 2. Requires a short walk through the creek (waterproof shoes recommended). Reservations required May-September.
attractions.entranceFee: Day use $12 per vehicle; free with reservation May-Sept

attractions.name: Trees of Mystery, Klamath
attractions.description: A quirky roadside attraction with gondola rides through the redwood canopy, Native American museum, and famous Paul Bunyan statue. A fun, kitschy stop on the drive north.

roadTripDays.tips: June is often foggy in San Francisco; late September and October typically offer the best weather along the entire route

hotels.name: Kimpton Hotel Monaco
hotels.notes: Stylish boutique in downtown Portland

hotels.name: Hotel Vance
hotels.notes: Solid mid-range in the Pearl District

attractions.name: Cannon Beach and Haystack Rock
attractions.description: Oregon's most iconic coastal town, home to the 235-foot Haystack Rock. Stroll the beach, browse galleries, and spot tufted puffins nesting in the rock during spring. The town inspired the setting for Goonies.

attractions.name: Multnomah Falls (Columbia River Gorge)
attractions.description: Oregon's tallest waterfall at 620 feet, a short drive east of Portland on the Columbia River Gorge Historic Highway. A 1.1-mile paved trail leads to the viewing bridge. One of the most visited natural attractions in the Pacific Northwest.
attractions.hours: Open daily, dawn to dusk; visitor center 9am-5pm
attractions.entranceFee: Free; parking reservation required May-October, $2 fee

attractions.name: Portland Food and Drink Scene
attractions.description: Portland is famous for its food carts (over 500 pods city-wide), craft beer scene, Powell's Books (world's largest independent bookstore), Voodoo Doughnut, and the Pearl District restaurants. A full evening of exploration awaits.
attractions.hours: Varies by venue; food carts typically 11am-9pm

roadTripDays.tips: Gas prices in California can be $0.50-$1.00 higher per gallon than the national average; fill up in Oregon where there are no self-serve pumps (attendants pump for you)

hotels.name: Hotel Monaco Seattle
hotels.notes: Boutique Kimpton near Pike Place Market

hotels.name: Hyatt House Seattle
hotels.notes: Great value, extended-stay style

hotels.name: Moore Hotel
hotels.notes: Budget historic hotel, steps from Pike Place

attractions.name: Mount St. Helens National Volcanic Monument (optional detour)
